dialogue.utterance: Unit 3, dispatched for shortness of breath at Richards in [CITY].
Hi ma'am, I'm Dr. Jess, a paramedic. What made you call 911 today?
Shortness of breath. It actually started about thirty minutes ago.
I'm just checking a few things to confirm you're awake. Are you aware of where you are?
I see you're opening your eyes normally. What city are we in?
Can you tell me the date today?
I want you to squeeze both of my fingers for me.
Your throat looks clear. Is it difficult to talk or feel muffled in any way?
Measuring your breathing now.
You have 22 breaths per minute with equal chest rise and fall. There is slightly increased work of breathing.
Picking up a strong pulse of 107. Your hands are warm, skin is dry, and there is good capillary refill.
Do you have any other symptoms like nausea?
No chest pain, just shortness of breath. I had a mild wave of nausea earlier though. I have a productive cough.
I need to ask about your medical history. You have a history of Primary Hypertension, Asthma-Mild, Non-Insulin Dependent Diabetes, COPD, and CHF. When did you use your Albuterol today?
About fifteen minutes ago. I tried it earlier too when I started coughing.

dialogue.role: nose, mouth and throat exam; Paramedic
dialogue.utterance: I see some mucus. Any pressure in your head?
Your skin looks warm and dry.
I need to listen to your breathing. Can you take a few slow breaths?
Breath sounds are clear and equal.
Pulse is 107, strong and regular.
Your extremities have good movement. Any discomfort in your back?
Blood pressure is 151 over 83.
SpO₂ is 98%.
Glucose is high at 341.
EKG now-Sinus Tachycardia noted.
Starting 4L oxygen through your nasal cannula. It should help with your breathing.
Placing you on 4 lead monitoring now.
Let's reassess your condition now.
Performing a 12 lead EKG now-to get a full picture of your heart activity.
Your pupils are equal, round, and reactive to light-PERRL. You are alert and oriented to person, place, time, and event-A&Ox4.
Your breathing is slightly increased at 24.
Pulse has dropped to 100.
Blood pressure is now 108 over 79.
SpO₂ is still at 99%.
EKG continues to show sinus tachycardia.
We'll perform capnography now to assess your carbon dioxide levels.
SpO₂ remains at 99%. Taking your temperature now.
Temperature is 99.1. Glucose is still at 341.
Your GCS is 15. Based on your shortness of breath, no chest pain, and sinus tachycardia, we will follow the respiratory distress protocol.
We'll be transporting you to [COMPANY_NAME] now.
We'll assist you to the stretcher now. Lie as flat as comfortable.
You're secure and comfortable. We're heading to the hospital now.
Preparing to handoff to the hospital. All notes and vitals are prepared.
Thank you, I feel a bit better now.

dialogue.role: eyes, ears, and nose exam; Paramedic
dialogue.utterance: Your pupils remain PERRL-alert and oriented with no neurological deficit.
You report shortness of breath (SOB), no chest pain, and mild nausea with productive coughing.
Glucose remains at 341 after reassessment.
EKG continues to be consistent with sinus tachycardia.
Reconfirming your medications-Coreg (carvedilol), Prinivil (lisinopril), and Glypizide (metformin), as well as Albuterol.
No signs of neck masses, swelling, or difficulty swallowing. No nuchal rigidity. No bite or sting marks noted.
We see you're not in evident pain. You mentioned shortness of breath (SOB) and a productive cough.
No rashes, petechia, or bruises-only normal skin color, warm, dry, and no capillary refill issues.

dialogue.role: nose, mouth and throat exam; Paramedic
dialogue.utterance: No signs of hearing loss, foreign body, obstruction, or sunken eyes. Nasal area normal.
No heart murmurs detected, bilateral breath sounds are equal with no adventitious sounds.
Abdominal assessment is deferred at this time.
Genitourinary assessment is deferred at this time.
No deformities or angulation noted. No vertebral column abnormalities reported.
On a scale of 0 to 10, your pain is a 0. No chest or back pain reported.
You have shortness of breath and a productive cough.
Pulse now at 100, rate decreased from earlier 107-warmer skin remains consistent with adequate perfusion.
Blood pressure 108/79 is within normal range.
Glucose remains slightly above normal, 341 again.
SpO₂ continues at 99% while on oxygen.
EKG still showing sinus tach with no pathological axis or ST changes.
No medications given during transport. You are prescribed Albuterol, Glypizide (metformin), and other baseline medications.
12 lead EKG performed again just before transport for confirmation, results repeat as nonspecific changes.

dialogue.role: eyes, ears, and nose exam; Paramedic
dialogue.utterance: Your pupils remain PERRL and your level of responsiveness remains high throughout the transport.
Skin assessment continues normal-still dry, warm, and consistent.
Bilateral breath sounds remain equal, unaffected, with no wheezing or crackles.
Your EKG continues clear of ventricular or atrial arrhythmias-sinus tach remains.
No neck rigidity-vitals and examinations consistent with COPD exacerbation.

dialogue.role: nose, mouth and throat exam; Paramedic
dialogue.utterance: Breathing unlabored despite history of asthma and COPD.
We've repeated the EKG for confirmation and double-checked oxygen and pulse readings en route.
Pain remains 0 throughout transport.
Despite high glucose, no symptoms of DKA observed.
No signs of paraphernalia or substance abuse consistent with clinical presentation.
No recent travel or contact with a sick individual reported.
You transferred from the vehicle to the stretcher without incident.
You were safely loaded into the ambulance during transport.
You were transferred from the EMS stretcher to a hospital cot upon arrival.
No issues noted during stretcher transition.
You're heading to [CITY] under the respiratory protocol based on findings in the field.
All medical documentation and device readings have been recorded in the patient care report.
Motor and sensory functions appear intact with no signs of neurological impairment.
Monitoring continued throughout transport-vitals remained stable without fluctuations.
SpO₂ remains at 99%, oxygen settings not altered during transport.
No new pain reported and no impact on management.
Coreg (carvedilol) and Prinivil (lisinopril) are your primary medications for hypertension.
Glypizide (metformin) and Albuterol are also part of your medication regimen.
You have a known history of COPD, mild asthma, CHF, diabetes, and hypertension.
You haven't stated your most recent solid food intake, but there is likely recent fluid intake with stable glucose levels.
No known allergies reported-no allergic reactions observed.
EKG findings unchanged showing a nonspecific T wave abnormality with no acute changes.
Capnography completed and displayed an end-tidal CO₂ of 30-within normal range for someone on oxygen.
No noticeable masses, swelling, or other abnormalities detected in the head and neck area.

dialogue.role: nose, mouth and throat exam; Paramedic
dialogue.utterance: No nasal drainage, or pharyngeal obstruction observed.
Lung exam remains clear, no indications of cardiac abnormality.
No signs of insect bites or trauma recorded during skin assessment.
Primary complaint: shortness of breath with a productive cough. No diarrhea or vomiting reported.
12 lead EKG result still reads with no acute cardiac compromise.
Current intervention remains unchanged-oxygen and monitoring continue.
The plan is to transport you in stable condition to [COMPANY_NAME] for further assessment.
All conditions support safe transport under the current protocol.
Assignment to room 6 provided to hospital staff at destination.
Preparing to assist in any transition to hospital stretcher equipment.
All medical details have been reviewed and passed along.
No vertebral discrepancies or discomfort.
All limb movements are normal and unimpaired.
No known surgical procedures reflected in clinical history.
Your current symptoms match the initial complaint-shortness of breath with productive cough.
Heart function remains unchanged from prior examinations.
EKG and pulse findings remain consistent throughout transport.
Diagnosed as likely a COPD exacerbation and possible common cold.
Patient confirmed as 63 years of age, female.
Oxygen saturation remained in a normal range during your transport.
Your pulse rate slightly decreased compared to the initial observation.
Your breathing remains elevated at 24 with consistent work of breathing.
